1: har hammôrîyāh — ‘Mount Moriah.’ This is the only place in the Bible where the temple mount is explicitly called Mount Moriah; the identification connects the temple site to Genesis 22:2, where God commanded Abraham to offer Isaac ‘on one of the mountains of which I shall tell you,’ and Genesis 22:14 where Abraham named the place ‘The LORD Will Provide (YHWH yirʾeh), so it is said to this day, “On the mount of the LORD it shall be provided (yērāʾeh).”’ The Chronicler’s identification of the temple site as Moriah is his contribution to the theological geography: the altar of burnt offering stands where the proto-sacrifice of the covenant son occurred. The ram that substituted for Isaac foreshadowed the temple’s constant burnt-offering ministry; the Son-offering on the mountain becomes the interpretive backdrop for all Solomonic sacrifice. The verse also specifies: ‘where YHWH had appeared to David his father, at the place that David had designated, on the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite.’ Three layers of sacred history converge: Abrahamic proto-sacrifice (Moriah), Davidic theophany (plague-stopping angel), and Jebusite-turned-Israelite purchase (Ornan). The MKT translates ‘Mount Moriah’ without explanation, letting the Genesis echo carry its own weight for the reader.
8: qōḏeš haqqŏḏāšîm — ‘the Most Holy Place’; literally ‘holy of holies’ — the Hebrew superlative by reduplication. The sanctuary’s inner chamber (Hebrew dᵉḇîr, also translated ‘inner sanctuary’ or ‘oracle room’) is given both its architectural name and its holiness-superlative. dᵉḇîr is etymologically uncertain: possibly from dāḇar (word/speak, = the place of the divine word) or from an Aramaic/Ugaritic root meaning ‘back, rear chamber.’ The HALOT prefers the latter: it is simply the rearmost room. The MKT uses ‘most holy place,’ the standard English rendering. The dimensions (20 x 20 cubits square) and the 600 talents of gold overlay (2 Chronicles 3:8) mark the superlative holiness with architectural scale and material extravagance. The gold of the Most Holy Place (600 talents) exceeds the gold of the rest of the temple (400 talents, 2 Chronicles 3:4) by 50% — proportional investment calibrated to the holiness gradient.
2: wayyāḥel liḇnôt baḥōḏeš haššēnî baššānāh hārᵉḇîʿît lᵉmalküttô — ‘He began construction in the second month of the fourth year of his reign.’ The dating formula — second month, fourth year — corresponds to the 1 Kings 6:1 dating: ‘in the four hundred and eightieth year after the people of Israel came out of Egypt, in the fourth year of Solomon’s reign over Israel, in the month of Ziv, which is the second month.’ The Chronicler’s abbreviated dating refers the reader to the 1 Kings chronological framework without repeating it. The second month (May-June) is after the spring rains, the optimal season for beginning major construction. The 480-year Exodus-to-temple reckoning (twelve generations of forty years) frames the temple-building as the culmination of Israel’s redemption-history trajectory.
3: wᵉʾēlleh hûssad šᵉlōmōh liḇnôt et-bêt hāʾelōhîm hāʾōreḵ ʾammôt bāammāh hārʾišōnāh — ‘This is how Solomon laid the foundations — the length, measured by the old standard, was sixty cubits.’ bāammāh hārʾišōnāh (in the cubit of the first/old standard) — the standard royal/long cubit of approximately 52-53 cm, as opposed to the shorter common cubit (~45 cm). The ‘old standard’ cubit is the same as the ‘cubit and a handbreadth’ of Ezekiel 40:5 and 2 Chronicles 43:13. The explicit note about the measurement standard is a technical precision reflecting the Chronicler’s concern for accurate transmission of the building’s dimensions — the house of God was built to known, verifiable standards, not arbitrary measurements.
4: wᵉhāʾûlām ʾăšer ʿal-pᵉnê hāʾōreḵ ʿal-pᵉnê rōḥab habbayit ʿeśrîm ammāh — ‘The vestibule in front of the main hall was twenty cubits wide — matching the width of the house — and a hundred cubits high.’ The vestibule (ʾûlām) is the entrance porch. Its height of 120 cubits (if the MT is read as it stands) is four times the height of the main hall (30 cubits) — an architectural striking element like a gatehouse tower. Many commentators emend to 20 cubits (following the LXX and the 1 Kings 6:3 parallel of ‘ten cubits’), but the MT’s 120-cubit height may reflect deliberate Chronicler hyperbole emphasizing the vestibule’s visibility and impressiveness as a gateway to the sacred space. The MKT translates ‘a hundred and twenty cubits high,’ following the MT.
5: wᵉʾet-habbayit haggāḏôl ḥippāh ʿēṣ bᵉrôšîm wayyᵉṣappēhû zāhāb ṭôb — ‘The main hall he paneled with cypress wood, then covered with fine gold, carving palm trees (tîmōrôt) and chains (rᵉtûqôt) into it.’ The two decorative motifs — palm trees and chain-work — are also found in Ezekiel’s vision-temple (Ezekiel 40:16,22,26,31,34,37; 2 Chronicles 41:18-20,25-26), suggesting a shared iconographic tradition for the ideal Israelite sanctuary. The palm tree (tāmār/tîmōrāh) is an image of uprightness and flourishing (Psalms 92:12: ‘the righteous will flourish like a palm tree’) and may represent the tree-of-life motif in the garden-temple. The gold overlay (zāhāb ṭôb, fine/good gold) transforms functional cedar paneling into liturgical art.
6: wayyᵉṣap et-habbayit ʾeben yᵉqārāh lᵉtipʾāret wᵉhazzāhāḇ zᵉhab pārwāyim — ‘He decorated the house with precious stones for beauty (lᵉtipʾāret), and the gold used was gold of Parvaim.’ tᵉpʾāret (beauty, adornment, glory) — the temple’s aesthetic quality is not incidental but theological: the sanctuary was to be beautiful because YHWH’s own glory (kāḇôḏ) is beautiful. pārwāyim is a gold-source of uncertain location — possibly in Arabia or southern Arabia. The Chronicler’s specification of the gold’s provenance serves the authenticating function of the narrative: this is real gold from a known source, not symbolic material. The total gold of the interior (600 talents for the Most Holy Place plus the gold of the main hall) represents an extraordinary material investment in the sanctuary’s holiness-visible expression.
7: wayyᵉṣap et-habbayit haqôrôt hasspîm wᵉqîrôtāyw wᵉdaltôtāyw zāhāb — ‘He overlaid the beams, thresholds, walls, and doors of the house with gold.’ The comprehensive gold-overlay covers every structural surface: qōrôt (beams/rafters), sippîm (thresholds), qîrôt (walls), dᵉlātôt (doors) — a total environment of gold. The comprehensive overlay answers the incomprehensibility announced in 2 Chronicles 2:6 (the heavens cannot contain YHWH) with extravagant material response: if we cannot adequately house the One who fills all, we can at least make the house as glorious as our best materials allow. wayyᵉpaqqēaḥ kᵉrûḇîm ʿal-haqqîrôt (and he carved cherubim on the walls) — the cherubim (kᵉrûḇîm) mark the walls as within the divine presence-zone; cherubim guard Eden’s entrance and stand at YHWH’s throne in Ezekiel’s visions.
9: ûmišqal hammaśmᵉrôt zāhāb ḥămiššîm šᵉqālîm — ‘The gold nails weighed fifty shekels.’ The specification of nail-weight (50 shekels = approximately 570 grams per nail) is a detail of extraordinary material luxury: even the structural fasteners were of gold. The Chronicler’s inclusion of this detail serves the accumulative rhetorical effect of the temple-building account: every element, including the most mundane construction hardware, participated in the gold-saturation of the sanctuary. wᵉʾet-hāʿăliyyôt ṣippāh zāhāb (he also overlaid the upper chambers with gold) — the upper chambers (ʿăliyyôt) are the upper stories of the temple structure; their gold-overlay continues the comprehensive gilding of the entire building interior.
10: wayyaʿaś bᵉḇêt qōḏeš haqqŏḏāšîm šᵉnê kᵉrûḇîm maʿăśēh ṣabbāʿ wayyᵉṣappû ʾōtām zāhāb — ‘In the Most Holy Place he carved two cherubim and overlaid them with gold.’ maʿăśēh ṣabbāʿ — the method of craftsmanship: ṣāḇaʿ may indicate overlay-work, carved and plated, or gilded sculpture. These two cherubim are distinct from the gold cherubim on the ark (Exodus 25:18-22), which were integral to the ark’s construction. The Most Holy Place’s cherubim are large free-standing figures (each ten cubits tall, wingspan ten cubits — 2 Chronicles 3:11) that spread their wings to cover the ark from above. The overall effect is a cherubim-canopy: the ark-seat of YHWH’s presence is sheltered beneath six layers of cherub-wings (two cherubim × two wings each × two sides).
11: kᵉnap hakkᵉrûḇîm hāʾēlleh pōrᵉśîm ammôt ʿeśrîm — ‘The combined wingspan of the cherubim was twenty cubits.’ The wingspan calculation: each cherub had two wings of five cubits each (total ten cubits per cherub); two cherubim = twenty cubits total wingspan — exactly spanning the width of the Most Holy Place (also twenty cubits, 2 Chronicles 3:8). The fit is architectural precision: the cherubim were sized to span the room completely, wing-tip to wing-tip. One wing of each cherub touched the outer wall; the other met the opposite cherub’s wing at the center. This creates a complete aerial covering over the ark — the mercy seat covered by cherub-wings — expressing the protective-presence theology of the tabernacle cherubim (Exodus 25:20).
12: wᵉkᵉnap hakkᵉrûḇ hāʾaḥēr ammôt ḥāmēš maggaʿat qîr habbayit — ‘And each wing of the second cherub was likewise five cubits, one touching the wall and the other meeting the first cherub’s wing.’ The symmetry is deliberate: both cherubim are identical in dimension, positioned symmetrically. The outer wings touch the walls; the inner wings meet at the center. The structural result is that the Most Holy Place is entirely sheltered by cherub-wings: no portion of the ceiling above the ark is uncovered. This architectural expression of the kappōret (mercy seat, cover of the ark) extended upward — the mercy-covering principle radiates from the ark’s surface to the entire room’s aerial space.
13: kᵉnpê hakkᵉrûḇîm hāʾēlleh pōrᵉśîm lᵉʿumat pᵉnêhem habbêtāh — ‘The wingspan of these cherubim spanned twenty cubits. They stood upright, facing the main hall.’ wᵉpᵉnêhem labbāyit (and their faces toward the house/main hall) — the cherubim face outward, toward the hêkāl (main hall) rather than inward toward each other. This orientation positions them as guardians facing the approach-direction, consistent with the cherubim’s guardian function at Eden’s entrance (Genesis 3:24). The priest entering the Most Holy Place would encounter the cherubim face-to-face — a visual representation of approaching YHWH’s guarded throne-space.
14: wayyaʿaś et-happārōket tᵉkēlet wᵉʾargāmān wᵉkarmîl ûḇûṣ wayyaʿal ʿālāyw kᵉrûḇîm — ‘He made the curtain of blue, purple, and crimson yarn and fine linen, with cherubim woven into it.’ The pārōket (curtain, veil) separating the Most Holy Place from the main hall is the temple’s most theologically charged textile: it marks the boundary between the holy and the most holy, the accessible and the inaccessible. The four-color specification — blue (tᵉkēlet), purple (ʾargāmān), crimson (karmîl), and fine linen (bûṣ) — matches the tabernacle veil exactly (Exodus 26:31-33), establishing the temple’s veil as the Mosaic tradition’s fulfillment. The Chronicler’s mention of the veil anticipates Matthew 27:51: at Christ’s death, the temple veil was torn from top to bottom, opening the way into the Most Holy Place that the high priest could only enter once a year.
15: wayyaʿaś lipnê habbayit šᵉtê ʿammûḏîm ʾōreḵ šᵉlōšîm wᵉḥāmēš ammāh — ‘In front of the temple he erected two pillars, each thirty-five cubits high, topped with a capital five cubits high.’ The two entrance pillars — Yākîn and Bōʿaz (2 Chronicles 3:17) — are the temple’s most visible architectural statement. Their height (35 cubits plus 5-cubit capitals = 40 cubits total, approximately 20 meters) made them visual landmarks across Jerusalem. The pillars did not support the roof structure (they stood separately before the vestibule) but served a monumental-symbolic function: marking the threshold of sacred space. The parallel in 1 Kings 7:15-22 gives 18-cubit heights (possibly measuring the shaft alone, without the base), suggesting the Chronicler’s figures include base and capital dimensions.
16: wayyaʿaś šarᵉšᵉrôt kaddᵉḇîr wayyittēn bᵉrōʾš hāʿammûḏîm wayyaʿaś rimmônim meʾāh — ‘He made chain-work like necklaces and set them on top of the pillars, with a hundred pomegranates hanging on each chain.’ rimmônim (pomegranates) — the pomegranate is a recurring sacred symbol in Israelite material culture: it decorated the hem of the high priest’s robe (Exodus 28:33-34), appears on the ark’s design, and ornaments the temple pillars. The pomegranate’s symbolism — multiple seeds within one fruit, associated with fertility and abundance — made it appropriate for the boundary-markers of sacred space. The hundred pomegranates per chain (total 400 per pillar in 1 Kings 7:20) represent the fullness of covenant blessing visually inscribed on the temple entrance.
17: wayyāqem et-hāʿammûḏîm ʿal-pᵉnê hahêkāl... wayyiqrāʾ šēm hayᵉmānî Yākîn wᵉšēm haśśᵉmālî Bōʿaz — ‘He erected the pillars in front of the temple — the southern one he named Jachin and the northern one Boaz.’ The two names carry interpretive weight: Yākîn (from kûn, to establish, make firm) = ‘He establishes’ or ‘it stands firm’; Bōʿaz (perhaps from bō, in + ʿōz, strength) = ‘in him is strength’. Together they may form a promise: YHWH establishes [the Davidic dynasty] by his strength — a covenantal statement inscribed at the temple entrance. The same pillar-names appear in Freemasonic tradition as foundational symbols, reflecting the pillars’ long afterlife as emblems of divine establishment and strength.
